name: rust-async-patterns
description: Master Rust async programming with Tokio, async traits, error handling, and concurrent patterns. Use when building async Rust applications, implementing concurrent systems, or debugging async code.
capabilities: Implement concurrent task execution with tokio::spawn and JoinSet
Design communication patterns using mpsc, broadcast, watch, and oneshot channels
Handle async errors with anyhow, thiserror, and timeout wrappers
Implement graceful shutdown using CancellationToken and signal handling
Create async traits and stream processors with proper bounds
Manage shared state with RwLock, Mutex, and Semaphore for connection pooling
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 51 static analysis findings are false positives. The skill contains documentation-only markdown files with Rust code examples. The static analyzer incorrectly flagged: markdown code fence backticks as shell execution, tokio::spawn (async task spawning) as process execution, SQL queries as system reconnaissance, and example URLs as hardcoded secrets. No executable code or security risks present.
valueStatement: Building async Rust applications requires understanding complex concurrency patterns. This skill provides production-ready Tokio patterns for tasks, channels, streams, and graceful shutdown handling.
limitations: Provides patterns and examples, not a complete async framework
Requires Rust programming knowledge and Cargo project setup
Focuses on Tokio runtime, not other async runtimes like async-std
Examples may require adaptation for specific use cases and dependencies

useCases.targetUser: Backend engineers building high-performance Rust services
useCases.title: Network Service Development
useCases.description: Build concurrent HTTP servers or WebSocket handlers using Tokio tasks and channels for request distribution and graceful shutdown.

useCases.targetUser: Data engineers implementing ETL pipelines in Rust
useCases.title: Data Pipeline Implementation
useCases.description: Process streaming data with async streams, buffer_unordered for controlled concurrency, and proper error propagation.

useCases.targetUser: Rust developers troubleshooting async runtime issues
useCases.title: Async Code Debugging
useCases.description: Diagnose deadlock issues, understand task cancellation, and implement proper tracing instrumentation for async applications.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Async Task Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me set up a basic Tokio async application with proper error handling. I need to fetch data from multiple URLs concurrently and handle any failures gracefully.

promptTemplates.title: Channel-Based Communication
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to implement a worker pool pattern where multiple producer tasks send work items to consumer tasks. Show me how to use mpsc channels with proper shutdown handling.

promptTemplates.title: Graceful Shutdown Implementation
promptTemplates.prompt: My async server needs to handle SIGINT/SIGTERM and shutdown cleanly. All in-flight requests should complete within 30 seconds before forced termination. Implement this with CancellationToken.

promptTemplates.title: Stream Processing with Backpressure
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm processing a large dataset as a stream. Implement chunked processing with buffer_unordered to limit concurrency to 10 items, with proper error handling for failed items.
promptTemplates.scenario: Advanced data processing with resource constraints

bestPractices: Use tokio::select! for racing futures and implementing cancellation points in long-running tasks
Prefer channels over shared state for task communication to avoid deadlocks and reduce lock contention
Instrument async functions with tracing attributes and respect CancellationToken for graceful shutdown
antiPatterns: Blocking async code with std::thread::sleep or blocking I/O operations that starve the runtime
Holding locks across await points which can cause deadlocks when multiple tasks contend for the same locks
Spawning unbounded tasks without semaphores or JoinSet limits leading to resource exhaustion

faq.question: What is the difference between tokio::spawn and std::thread::spawn?
faq.answer: tokio::spawn creates lightweight async tasks scheduled by the Tokio runtime, while std::thread::spawn creates OS threads. Tokio tasks are cheaper and can be cancelled, but require async/await syntax.

faq.question: When should I use channels vs shared state (Mutex/RwLock)?
faq.answer: Use channels for message passing between tasks to avoid lock contention and deadlocks. Use shared state when tasks need true shared memory access or for caching frequently-read data.

faq.question: How do I handle timeouts in async Rust?
faq.answer: Use tokio::time::timeout() to wrap futures with a duration. It returns Result<Result<T, InnerError>, Elapsed> where Elapsed indicates the timeout was exceeded.

faq.question: What is the purpose of async-trait crate?
faq.answer: Rust does not natively support async fn in traits. The async-trait crate provides a procedural macro that transforms async trait methods into boxed futures, enabling async polymorphism.

faq.question: How can I debug async Rust applications?
faq.answer: Use tokio-console with RUSTFLAGS='--cfg tokio_unstable' for runtime task monitoring. Add tracing spans with #[instrument] and use tracing-subscriber for structured logging.

faq.question: What is buffer_unordered and when should I use it?
faq.answer: buffer_unordered(n) limits concurrent processing of stream items to n at a time. Use it to prevent resource exhaustion when processing many items concurrently, such as rate-limited API calls.
